Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts L-serine and acetyl-CoA into O-acetylserine, the activated carbon/nitrogen precursor for cysteine formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SERAT1;1 contributes to reduced SERAT activity in the serat triple knockout, causing cysteine synthesis depletion, metabolic reprogramming, and reduced growth; external sugar can partially rescue downstream TOR-associated growth defects.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genotyping of serat tko; SERAT enzyme activity assay; metabolite profiling/PCA; fresh-weight phenotyping; sugar-feeding rescue assays.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hydroponic culture on 1/2 Hoagland medium; short-day growth; sulfur-sufficient versus sulfur-restricted supply; glucose or sucrose feeding.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OAS-TL (used in coupled SERAT activity assay)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This gene was analyzed as part of the serat tko genotype together with SERAT2;1 and SERAT2;2; the paper emphasizes combined depletion rather than isoform-specific dissection.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: O-acetylserine formation branch of cysteine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced biomass and altered primary metabolism when SERAT capacity is strongly lowered.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for cysteine precursor supply; rate-limiting status not explicitly established here
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SERAT1;1 is one of the disrupted SERAT genes in the cysteine synthesis-depleted serat tko mutant. 2. Reduced combined SERAT activity lowers cysteine synthetic capacity and perturbs metabolism and growth. 3. Sugar supply partially restores growth-associated outputs downstream of this metabolic defect.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Sulfur-containing amino acid required for protein synthesis and precursor of glutathione and other sulfur metabolites.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts L-serine and acetyl-CoA into O-acetylserine for downstream cysteine formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SERAT2;1 as part of serat tko lowers total SERAT activity, contributing to cysteine synthesis depletion and growth inhibition.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genotyping of serat tko; SERAT enzyme activity assay; metabolite profiling/PCA; biomass measurements; sugar-feeding rescue.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hydroponic 1/2 Hoagland medium; short-day conditions; sulfur supply variation; glucose or sucrose feeding.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OAS-TL (used in coupled SERAT activity assay)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function was investigated in a triple mutant context, not as an isolated single-gene study.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: O-acetylserine formation branch of cysteine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong reduction impairs growth and shifts metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key precursor-supply step; explicit rate limitation not directly proven
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SERAT2;1 is a constituent of the serat tko mutant used to deplete cysteine synthesis. 2. Loss of combined SERAT isoforms reduces O-acetylserine-producing activity. 3. This contributes to metabolic and growth defects linked to impaired cysteine production.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports sulfur amino acid metabolism and glutathione biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts L-serine and acetyl-CoA into O-acetylserine, which is then used for cysteine synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SERAT2;2 in the serat tko contributes to decreased SERAT activity, cysteine synthesis depletion, and growth defects.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genotyping of serat tko; SERAT enzyme activity assay; metabolite profiling/PCA; fresh-weight measurements; sugar-feeding rescue assays.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hydroponics; short-day growth; sulfur-replete and sulfur-restricted conditions; glucose/sucrose supplementation.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OAS-TL (used in coupled SERAT activity assay)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Studied only in combination with SERAT1;1 and SERAT2;1 as the serat tko mutant.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: O-acetylserine formation branch of cysteine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe activity loss reduces biomass and perturbs metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key precursor-supply step; bottleneck role not individually resolved
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SERAT2;2 is one of three SERAT genes knocked out in the serat tko line. 2. Combined SERAT deficiency lowers cysteine biosynthetic capacity. 3. The resulting metabolic limitation is associated with reduced growth and altered downstream signaling outputs.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Building block for proteins and precursor of glutathione and sulfur metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Sulfur assimilation / reductive sulfate assimilation leading to cysteine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces sulfite to sulfide, supplying the sulfur donor required for O-acetylserine conversion to cysteine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced SiR activity depletes cysteine synthesis, triggers transcriptional upregulation of sulfate uptake/assimilation genes, represses growth and ribosome-associated functions, lowers TOR activity, and causes strong biomass defects that can be partially rescued by glucose or sucrose.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant genotyping; immunodetection of SiR protein; metabolite profiling/PCA; microarray and qRT-PCR validation; biomass and grafting phenotypes; TOR activity assay via S6K phosphorylation; sugar-feeding rescue; sulfide fumigation.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Hydroponic 1/2 Hoagland medium; short-day conditions; regular versus sulfur-restricted supply; glucose or sucrose feeding; sulfide fumigation; soil or agar media; TOR inhibitor AZD8055; antimycin A.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: sir1-1, sir1-3 and sir1-4 are reduced-function lines used to show that impaired sulfite reduction has stronger effects on growth and TOR than the serat tko line; sulfide fumigation supports the conclusion that sulfur reduction products can stimulate sugars/TOR.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Reductive sulfate assimilation branch producing sulfide for cysteine formation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe SiR reduction causes strong shoot/root growth defects, reduced protein biosynthesis signatures, and broad metabolic disturbance.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for sulfur assimilation flux under the tested conditions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Limiting SiR strongly impairs sulfur assimilation and cysteine synthesis capacity. 2. sir mutants show compensatory induction of sulfate uptake/assimilation genes but decreased growth, ribosome-related transcripts, and TOR activity. 3. Sugar supply or reduced sulfur input can partially bypass downstream growth defects, linking sulfur assimilation to glucose-TOR signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides reduced sulfur for proteins and glutathione and supports growth and metabolic homeostasis.

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: TOR activity decreases when cysteine synthesis is impaired, and glucose re-activates TOR signaling to rescue growth and root meristem activity in sulfur-assimilation-defective plants.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vivo TOR activity assayed by S6K phosphorylation ratio; pharmacological inhibition with AZD8055; glucose feeding; root meristem EdU staining; biomass rescue assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller linking sulfur-derived metabolic status and glucose availability to growth outputs associated with cysteine sufficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Glucose feeding; sucrose feeding; sulfur sufficiency or restriction; sulfide fumigation; TOR inhibitor AZD8055; antimycin A; short-day growth.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Acts within glucose-TOR signaling responsive to metabolic inputs from sulfur assimilation; explicit feedback to cysteine pathway genes not established.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Growth signaling, translation/ribosome-related processes, and root meristem proliferation in response to sulfur and carbon status
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper primarily uses TOR activity as a mechanistic node explaining growth defects in cysteine synthesis-depleted mutants rather than demonstrating direct transcriptional regulation of sulfur assimilation genes by TOR.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: TOR inhibition decreases growth and can induce autophagy; direct manipulation may cause broad pleiotropic effects.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: S6K; growth and meristem activity outputs
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: TOR kinase complex implied; specific plant complex components not detailed in the provided text
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes growth-associated translation outputs as reflected by S6K phosphorylation; indirect positive effect on ribosome/protein synthesis status
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TOR activity is reduced in sir mutants with impaired sulfur assimilation/cysteine synthesis. 2. Glucose restores TOR activity and partially rescues biomass and root meristem defects. 3. TOR therefore mediates the downstream growth response to sulfur precursor availability.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Reduced sulfur amino acid required for glutathione synthesis and growth-supporting metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Glucose; sucrose; sulfur precursor availability; sulfide-derived metabolic status

Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GCN2 activity, monitored by eIF2α phosphorylation, is not strongly implicated as the main mediator of sulfur-assimilation-dependent cysteine/glutathione changes in the tested mutants and conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Immunodetection of phosphorylated eIF2α; chlorsulfuron positive control; metabolite measurement of cysteine and glutathione in gcn2 mutant under sulfur-sufficient and sulfur-deficient conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Not established as decisive; tested mainly to exclude a major role in the observed cysteine/glutathione and growth phenotype.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Regular versus sulfur-deficient nutrient supply; chlorsulfuron treatment as positive control.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a candidate amino acid stress regulator, but the supplementary data support that the major phenotype is better explained by glucose-TOR signaling than by GCN2 activation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because GCN2 broadly regulates translational stress responses, manipulation may have pleiotropic stress-related consequences.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirect translational repression via eIF2α phosphorylation; no decisive effect on target metabolic genes demonstrated here
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GCN2 activity was assayed as a possible sulfur/cysteine stress signaling component. 2. Under the tested conditions, GCN2 was not shown to be the principal driver of the cysteine synthesis depletion phenotype. 3. The study instead favors glucose-TOR signaling as the main downstream route affecting growth.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cysteine supports sulfur metabolism; glutathione is a major antioxidant and sulfur reservoir.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Amino acid stress; sulfur deficiency; chlorsulfuron
